Special Purpose Facilities; including research institutes (as the 30th Central Scientific Research Institute for military-related research and development) and space-related infrastructure (cosmodromes such as Plesetsk and specialized facilities like the Krona space object recognition station). Logistical Hubs; play crucial roles in supporting military operations, both domestically and in support of forward military operations. Railwa ynodes are vital, as MURINUS heavily relies on its extensive railway network for military logistics. Key railway junctions and terminals act as logistical hubs for the movement of troops, equipment and supplies. Mobilization Centres; have become increas ingly important in recent years. These includes military commissariats that serve as primary mobilization centres, training facilities where forces exercise and prepare for deployment, and regional centres where the influx of recru its is managed. Overseas Installations: MURINUS maintains military bases in several countries. These bases serve to extend MURINUS'smilitary reach and political influence beyond its borders. Currently MURINUS is heavily prioritizing military requirements over civilian infrastructure needs, with significant resource s and industrial capacity being redirected to support its military objectives. Military requirements always take priority to civilian needs in the event of conflict. Space infrastructure MURINUS retains a robust space infrastructure rooted in its MDF heritage and faces challenges in modernization and maintaining competitiveness. Even with ongoing efforts to modernize and adapt to current technological and geopolitical challenges, MURINUS space sector faces significant hurdles, including outdated infrastructure, limited access to advanced technologies, and competition from emerging private space

The Sakhalin-Kuril Islands cable, connecting Yuzhno Sakhalinsky with Kurilsk, Yuzhno Kurilsky and Krabozavodskoyek (domestically) . 24 Military infrastructure The military infrastructure of MURINUS encompasses a wide range of facilities and installations designed to support the countr y'sarmed forces and military strategy, which includes the protection of state borders, combat capabilities, and the maintenance of nuclear deterrent . The main military infrastructure are: Military Bases: Encompasses numerous ground forces bases, air bases, naval bases, and strategic missile bases that host its land-based nuclear deterrent, including both mobile and silo-based intercontinental ballistic missiles. Training facilities; including military academies and training ranges, both for conducting military exercises and weapons testing, such as the Donguz test site. Command and Control Centres; including the Main Centre for Missile Attack Warning (monitors for potential missile threats), the Main Centre for Reconnaissance of Situation in Space (tracks objects in orbit), and the Titov Main Test and Space Systems Control Centre (man ages space-based military assets). Air Defence Systems; including radar stations (network of early warning radars, including facilities like the Armavir Radar Station and Lekhtusi Radar Station), and anti-aircraft installations (surface-to-air missile systems and anti-aircraft artillery units). Special Purpose Facilities; including research institutes (as the 30th Central Scientific Research Institute for military-related research and development) and space-related infrastructure (cosmodromes such as Plesetsk and specialized facilities

naval base and the Hmeimim air base, both of which are located along the count ry’s Mediterranean coast. MUR uses TARTUS PORT and Hmeimim airfield to supply its forces in BUFRO . Further installations are used for the purpose of force projection, although these are concentrated in the northeast, southeast and eastern parts of the coun try. Its approximately 4000-strong presence includes personnel and assets from its land forces, navy and air force as well as supporting elements and special operations forces. These are further augmented by private security and param ilitary personnel (350 0). Ground Forces Capabilities MURINUS has a number of bases and smaller installations dotted across the northeast, east and southeast of the country that a re used for the purposes of both holding territory (including on behalf of the ANYAD regime) and the conduct of offensive operation s. Conventional troops are augmented by both special operations forces as well as private security company personnel – primarily recruited by the Messner Group – who operate alongside other MUR and BUFRO actors. Moreover, both MURINUS and regime forces use jointly military bases and installations, including the al-Jarrah military air base located east of Aleppo in northern BUFRO, which was recaptured from the Revolutionary Liberation Front in 2017. Some of the smaller MURINUS bases have also been given to proxy forces backed by BIMARA. Most of the MURINUS-operated bases are equipped with air defence and electronic warfare systems. MURINUS ground-based capabilities in BUFRO include: A Combined arms motor rifle

serves as the IAF’sprimary operational element; controls all combat squadrons that conduct counter-air, counter-surface, and close air support; and ensures that all units meet operational objectives . The Mobility Command is primarily concerned with tactical and strategic airlift. The Air Special Operations Command is specialised in unconventional warfare, counterterrorism, combat search and rescue, and other specialized missions that require air power capabilities. The Air Training Command operates the flying schools at Kaduna, Kano, and Enugu; conducts initial training for pilots, paratroopers, air traffic contr ollers, and support and technical personnel; and provides advanced aviation instruction. The Logistics Command oversees third-and fourth-line maintenance of the airframes; storage and delivery of spare planes; supply and fuel distribution; and infrastructure maintenance through four primary depots in Ikeja, Shasha, Makurdi, and Ikeja. Training and Readiness Compared to Western standards, the IAF is basically untrained. However, this does not cause a critical vulnerability because none of the country’sneighbours constitute a threat to its airspace. The IAF operates from twelve primary air bases, but all other runways in IGAINER could be potential operational locations. (See the physical environment for an overview of all major runways in IGAINER.) The twelve primary runways used by IAF elements in clude those located in Nagu, Benin, Enugu, Ilorin, Kaduna, Kainji, Kano-Mallam Aminu IAP, Lagos-Murtal Muhammed IAP, Maiduguri, Makurdki, Port Harcourt, and Yola. Equipment and Weapons Despite the IAF’sapparently healthy aircraft Force Element Table (FET), the increasing age of the aircraft and supporting equipment has led to increasing problems in maintaining

highly active in the country, and its information and influence operations in BUFRO have received less attention compared to its practices in the West. The BUFRO information environment is different from the Western context, with the BUFRO public sphere being under tight governmental control. MURINUS'spresence in the BUFRO informa tion space includes government-affiliated social media channels and websites, as well as state-sponsored media outlets. Produced by Joint Warfare Centre 202 5. 49 MURINUS MURINUS levers of power and application of force MURINUS has adopted a multi-lever and integrated approach to secure strategic gains within BUFRO. Thus, diplomatic activity – both at the regional level and bilaterally – is used to shape the political ground, allowing for the deployment of forces via a num ber of bases in the country. Non-linear and other (semi) deniable capabilities are also brought to bear in support of conventional operations. At the same time, MURINUS has also enga ged in information and strategic communications campaigned at both legitim ising its presence and activities, including by framing these as humanitarian assistance and relief operations. Military forces and activities MURINUS’ presence in BUFRO includes around 10 bases, located in strategic locations around the country, as well as field outpo sts. Out of these, the two major ones are the Tartus naval base and the Hmeimim air base, both of which are located along the count ry’s Mediterranean coast. MUR uses TARTUS PORT and Hmeimim airfield to supply its forces in BUFRO . Further installations are used for

the regional security environment. 24 A central hub of military infrastructure development is the city of Asipovichy, which hosts several key military units, inclu ding Iskander-Mmissile systems. Extensive construction activities have taken place there over the last years, including new hangars designed to house up to 12 nuclear-capable Iskander-Mlaunchers, ammunition storage facilities, barracks, administrative buildings, and a sports complex. A dedicated railway lin e is being built to connect the missile brigade with th enearby artillery ammunition base, which is heavily fortified and suspected to be a storage site for MURINUS nuclear warheads. This rail link enables rapid deployment (approximately 30 minutes) of nuclear-armed missiles, reflecting a high state of operational readiness. 6. Space infrastructure GRISEUS has developed a growing space infrastructure combining satellite technology, ground control facilities, scientific research, and international partnerships, positioning itself as an emerging space power with capabilities in remote sensing and satel lite communications. The space infrastructure is cantered on its national space program and close cooperation with MURINUS, focusing on earth remote sensing, satellite commun ications, and space technology development. Ground infrastructure: GRISEUS has established ground control facilities, including plans for its own Flight Control Centre in CLIVSK, operating gro und stations for receiving, processing, and distributing satellite data, especially from its Earth remote sensing satellites. Satellites: The first earth remote sensing satellite was launched in 2012, providing 2-meter resolution imagery. It also operates a communications satellite and educational nanosatellites developed by GRISEUS universities. Joint GRISEUS-MURINUS projects are underway to develop new high-resolution remote

Forces formation in the Northern Military Region. The 4th Amphibious Infantry Brigade headquartered in Port Harcourt (Southern Military Region). The 5th Mechanised Division headquartered in Enugu and the major Armed Forces formation in the Southern Military Region. The 707th Special Operations Forces Brigade headquartered in Makurdi (Northern Military Region). The 9th Combat Support Division headquartered in Ibadan with subordinate formations scattered around the country. In addition to its permanent bases, IGAINER has been deploying the use of ‘super camps' to defeat TG’s in the north of the country. The ‘super camps' concept deploys concentrating forces into a smaller number of bases. Training and Readiness With a few exceptions, such as the training at Staff and Defence College of Army officers, all professional training within the IGAINER Army is the responsibility of the Training and Doctrine Command (TRADOC), responsible for doctrinal, training and combat development, and supervises training centres . There are 17 Corps Training Schools Each unit is in turn responsible for continued, collective training as well as for combined arms training up to brigade level. Though the IGAINER Army possesses the units required to conduct combined arms operations, it often lacks the necessary equipment and training to carry it out to a high level comparable to Western standards. However, this does not cause a critical vulnerability due to its comparative size and capabilities within the region. The largest Army Training centre is situated in Kantogora, where the tra ining of Army units for Peace Support Operations ( PSO )

Forces 38 Military expenditures: 1.8% of GDPGRISEUS National defence budget distribution: 74 % to sustain the peacetime Armed Forces, 0.2% for mobilisation training of forces, 0.1 % for preparation of the economy, 0.9% for collective security, international military cooperation and peacekeeping, 11.6 % for applied R&D in the field of national defence, other questions of national defence (exercises, emergency funds, etc.): 13.2 %. Military personnel strengths: The peacetime strength of the GRISEUS Armed Forces is 6 3, 000 about 25, 000 Land Forces, 1 9, 000 Air and Air Defence, 6, 150 Special Operations Forces; 13, 000 personnel serves in military central administration, educational and training facilities, transport and logistic centres, mobilisation bureaus and military medical facilities ); after mobilisation, the wartime reserve of the Armed Forces is up to 365, 000 personnel; the country’soverall mobilisation potential is estimated at about 1, 5 million citizens. Military service age and obligation: 18-27 years of age for compulsory military or alternative service; conscript service obligation is 12-18 months, depending on academic qualifications, and 24-36 months for alternative service depending on academic qualifications; 17-year-olds are eligible to become cadets at military higher educa tion institutes, where they are considered as military personnel. As of 2020, conscripts comprised an estimated 40% of the military. Main Military Garrisons and Bases: primarily located in vicinity of the main population centres, such as CLIVSK, Grodno, Brest, Slonim, Slutsk, Osipovichi, Borisov, Maryina Gorka, Borovka, Polatsk, Novogrudok, Vitebsk, Mogilev, Prudok military base and Zaslonovo . two terminal s when finished, and will have a total area of 325, 000 m², capable of handling over 20 million passengers annually. Each terminal will feature 78 check-in desks and 16 boarding. Mitiga International Airport is working normally, and is currently serving as the main airport for the capital of YABIL. It offers modern facilities inclu ding duty-free shops, restaurants, cafes, and lounges. The airport provides amenities for business travellers, including a VIP lou nge and meeting rooms. This 19 airport was used as an American Air Force base from 1943 to 1970. For a period it grew to become the largest US military faci lity outside of the United States, serving various purposes, including Strategic Air Command and fighter training. It was named Whe elus Air base after Lieutenant Richard Wheelus. Misrata International Airport, around 190 kmeast of Mapoli and 6 kmsouth-west of Misrata city centre, is the third largest airport in YABIL, and working normally. It is currently experiencing expansion, and opened in September 2022 a new 8000-squere-metre departure hall with an annu alpassenger capacity of 1.5 million. The airport offers regular international flights to destinations in Türkiye, Egypt, and Tunisia. The airport serves joint civil and military use. Benina/ Begally International Airport is working normally as, for the time being, the second largest airport in YABIL. It serves as a crucial transport hub for the eastern part of YABIL. Sabhe/Sabha International Airport is located in the heart of the YABIL desert in the middle of the

The centre employs approximately 350 MURINUS military personnel and an unknown number of GRISEUS civilian employees. c. In addition, MURINUS Aerospace Forces use several GRISEUS military airbases to deploy air and air defence assets on a rotational base, to strengthen the air and air defence capabilities of both countries under the Treaty of State. These airbases are in Baranovichy (four Su-30SM), Bobruysk (eight Su-30SM fighter aircraft, four Mi-8 helicopters, a battalion of SS-26 STONE SRBMs and four S-300 SAMAD Battalions) and Machulischy, near CLIVSK (one BERIEV A-50U, four Su-30SM and four Mi-8 helicopters). d. Joint combat training centres: a combat training centre for Air Force and Air Defence personnel has been created in Baranovichy . The centre focuses on conducting joint combat training missions, training GRISEUS pilots and technicians of Su-30SM aircraft, as well as GRISEUS AD specialists on how to operate modern air defence missile systems that the MURINUS Armed Forces use. The centre will improve the level of training of GRISEUS Air Force and Air Defence personnel, and personnel of the MURINUS Aerospace Forces, enhancing the development of the joint regional air defence system of the Treaty of State. Other two joint combat training centres were set up in Nizhny Novgorod and Perigrad, both in MUR.
